content.problemStatement:
Dave recently mastered the problem of placing N queens on a chessboard so
that no two queens attack eachother.
Now he wants to see how many knights he can place on a chessboard so
that no two knights attack eachother.
Normally this would be a simple task, but some of the squares of the
chessboard have been marked as unusable and hence cannot have a knight placed
on them.

Recall that a knight can attack another knight if their vertical distance
is 2 and their horizontal distance is 1, or if their vertical distance is 1 and
their horizontal distance is 2.  Only one knight may be placed on each square
of the chessboard
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case begins with a line containing two integers M and N, denoting the number of rows and columns of the chessboard. M lines follow, each containing exactly N characters. The j-th character of the i-th line is '.' if a knight may be placed in the j-th column of the i-th row, and '#' if the square is unusable.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a single line the maximum number of knights that may be placed on the chessboard such that no two attack each other.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a backtracking approach. We start by initializing an empty board and then try to place a knight in each position. If it's safe to place a knight in a position, we mark it as occupied and recursively try to place knights in the remaining positions. If we can't place a knight in any position, we backtrack and try a different position.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M*N*8^(M*N)) per test case, O(T*M*N*8^(M*N)) total. The time complexity is exponential due to the backtracking approach.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M*N) — the dominant structure is the board.
